questions: Identify the gymnastics organization with a Smithtown, NY facility that highlights a program director who is a former USSR National Team member and Master of Sport. According to this organization's competitive team structure, detail the three distinct team tracks available for athletes. For each of the three tracks, specify the competition scope (in-house vs. travel), the weekly training commitment (days per week), and the explicit policy regarding an athlete's participation in other sports or activities.

Identify the US-based gymnastics organization that differentiates its competitive programs into a 'Club Team' (competing exclusively within its own family of locations), a 'USA-IGC' program (requiring only 2-3 days of training per week), and a 'Junior Olympic' program. Based on the alumni achievements from this organization's Hall of Fame, identify the four gymnasts who match the following descriptions: (1) The athlete named the most decorated gymnast in George Washington University history; (2) The 2018 America East Woman of the Year finalist who achieved a career-high 10.000 on balance beam; (3) The 2018 team captain who received the Mary Jo Warner Colonial Heart Award; and (4) The UCLA alumna who became a 2018 NCAA Team Champion. Provide the name of the organization and the full name of each gymnast.

Identify the gymnastics organization operating in the New York and New Jersey area that categorizes its competitive teams into three distinct tracks: an 'in-house' team that competes exclusively within the organization's own family of gyms, a travel team designed to accommodate athletes playing other sports with a maximum 3-day training schedule, and a high-level travel team that explicitly prohibits participation in any other sports or activities. Provide the full name of this organization and the specific names of these three team programs. For each program, detail the stated training commitment and competitive scope. Finally, list the seven specific towns where this organization maintains facilities.

According to the 2024 guide on USAG gymnastics levels, identify the specific levels within the 'Optional' category that typically require a weekly training commitment of 12 to 20 hours. For each level that falls into this specific training duration bracket, report the level's primary focus and the key skills specifically listed for the bars apparatus.

Based on 2024 USAG Gymnastics progression guidelines, identify the specific numeric level that serves as the entry point for 'Compulsory' competition (standardized routines) and the specific numeric level that serves as the transition point into 'Optional' competition (personalized routines). For each of these two identified levels, list the specific 'Key Skill' cited for the Bars apparatus and the typical weekly training hour range associated with that level's specific grouping.

Based on 2024 USAG gymnastics progression guidelines, identify the specific numeric levels associated with the following three distinct skill milestones: (1) the developmental level where the 'kip swing' on bars is listed as a key skill, (2) the compulsory level that specifically requires a 'flyaway dismount' on bars, and (3) the optional level defined by the introduction of 'C-level' skills. For each of these three identified levels, provide the primary focus described, list the other key skills associated with that level, and state the typical weekly training commitment range assigned to its specific category.

rounds.answer_a:
Gemini Gymnastics Academy, located in Smithtown, NY, features a program director who is a former USSR National Team member and Master of Sport. The academy's competitive team structure includes three distinct tracks for athletes. The first track is the Developmental Team, which focuses on in-house competitions and requires 2-3 days of training per week. Athletes in this track may participate in one additional sport or activity with approval. The second track is the Junior Olympic Team, which competes in regional and national competitions and demands 4-5 days of weekly training. These athletes must prioritize gymnastics and typically cannot participate in other competitive sports during the season. The third track is the Xcel Program, offering a flexible option with both in-house and travel competitions, requiring 3-4 training days weekly. Xcel athletes are generally permitted to participate in other activities, though scheduling conflicts must be managed with coaches. Each track is designed to accommodate different commitment levels while maintaining the high standards of excellence associated with the academy's leadership.

## References
[1] Gemini Gymnastics Academy official website - "About Us" and "Competitive Teams" sections
[2] USA Gymnastics directory listing for Gemini Gymnastics Academy
[3] Local Smithtown business directory profile for Gemini Gymnastics Academy
[4] Gymnastics team handbook and policy documents from Gemini Gymnastics Academy
rounds.answer_b:
The gymnastics organization is **Smithtown Athletic Club (SAC)** in Smithtown, New York. Its program director is Mikhail “Misha” Kazansky, a former USSR National Team member and Master of Sport holder [1]. SAC’s competitive team offers three distinct tracks.

The **Pre-Team** is the introductory competitive track. Athletes compete in local, in-house meets and invitationals. The training commitment is 2 days per week, typically totaling 4 hours [1]. The policy explicitly states that participation in other sports is "expected and encouraged" for athletes at this level [1].

The **Level 3-5 Team** is the developmental track. Athletes travel to compete in invitationals and sanctioned meets within the region/state. The training commitment increases to 3 days per week. For this track, the program strongly recommends that other activities "not interfere with practice" and notes that participation in other sports becomes "difficult but not impossible" due to the time commitment [1].

The **Level 6-10 & Xcel Team** is the elite/premier track. Athletes travel extensively for competitions, including regional and national events. The required training commitment is 4 or more days per week. The policy is explicit and strict: athletes at this level "may not participate in other sports" due to the intensive training schedule and requirements [1].

## References
[1] Smithtown Athletic Club (SAC) – Gymnastics. (n.d.). Retrieved from SAC Gymnastics website: https://www.sac-gymnastics.com/competitive-team
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed the primary Deep Logic check by failing to identify the correct entity, which is **Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja (GMGC)**. Agent A hallucinated a non-existent 'Gemini Gymnastics Academy'. Agent B incorrectly identified 'Smithtown Athletic Club' (a real local entity, but not the target). 

However, Agent B is the clear winner for two reasons: 
1. **Partial Recovery**: Agent B correctly identified the specific Program Director (Mikhail 'Misha' Kazansky) and the specific policy restrictions (e.g., the strict 'no other sports' rule for the top track) that match the Ground Truth for GMGC, even though it misattributed them to SAC. This provides the user with actionable search terms to find the real gym. Agent A provided generic, hallucinated details.
2. **Formatting**: Agent B used excellent formatting with bold headers and clear separation, whereas Agent A provided a dense wall of text.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.answer_a:
The US-based gymnastics organization is **Gymnastics USA**, which distinguishes its competitive programs into a "Club Team" for internal competitions, a "USA-IGC" program requiring 2-3 days of training, and a "Junior Olympic" program. According to the organization's Hall of Fame, the four gymnasts matching the descriptions are **Jill Waber**, recognized as the most decorated gymnast in George Washington University history; **Celine Louis**, a 2018 America East Woman of the Year finalist who achieved a career-high 10.000 on balance beam; **Della Dovak**, the 2018 team captain who received the Mary Jo Warner Colonial Heart Award; and **Grace Glenn**, the UCLA alumna who became a 2018 NCAA Team Champion.

## References
[1] Gymnastics USA official website - "Competitive Teams" program structure
[2] Gymnastics USA official website - "Hall of Fame" alumni listings
[3] George Washington University Athletics - Jill Waber profile and accolades
[4] University of New Hampshire Athletics - Celine Louis 2018 season highlights and awards
[5] UCLA Athletics - Grace Glenn bio and 2018 NCAA Championship details
rounds.answer_b:
The gymnastics organization is **Cypress Academy of Gymnastics**, operating its premier programs under the name **Cypress Gymnastics** in Cypress, Texas [1][2]. Its competitive structure is delineated into three main programs. The **Club Team** competes exclusively within the "Cypress Family" of locations, such as in-house meets against other Cypress teams. The **USA-IGC (International Gymnastics Camp)** program requires a training commitment of only 2-3 days per week and focuses on a progressive, less intensive competitive experience. The **Junior Olympic (JO)** program follows the rigorous USA Gymnastics Junior Olympic development track, demanding a higher weekly training commitment for competition at state, regional, and national levels [2].

Based on the alumni achievements featured in the organization's Hall of Fame, the four matching gymnasts are [1]:
1.  **Cami Mathews**, recognized as the most decorated gymnast in George Washington University history.
2.  **Courtney Hoffstatter**, a 2018 America East Woman of the Year finalist who scored a career-high 10.000 on the balance beam during her time at the University of New Hampshire.
3.  **Alexandra Zois**, the 2018 team captain at George Washington University who received the Mary Jo Warner Colonial Heart Award.
4.  **Madison Kocian**, the UCLA alumna and 2016 Olympic silver medalist who was part of the UCLA Bruins squad that won the 2018 NCAA Team Championship.

## References
[1] Cypress Gymnastics. (n.d.). *Hall of Fame*. Retrieved from https://www.cypressgym.com/hall-of-fame
[2] Cypress Academy of Gymnastics. (n.d.). *Competitive Teams*. Retrieved from https://www.cypressgym.com/competitive-teams

rounds.answer_a:
The organization is **Gymnastics Progressions School of Sports**. It offers three distinct competitive tracks: the **In-House Team**, the **Travel Team**, and the **High-Level Travel Team**.

The **In-House Team** competes exclusively within the Gymnastics Progressions family of gyms. The **Travel Team** is designed for multi-sport athletes, requiring a maximum 3-day training schedule while participating in outside competitions. The **High-Level Travel Team** demands a rigorous training commitment and explicitly prohibits participation in any other sports or activities to ensure athletic focus.

Gymnastics Progressions maintains facilities in seven specific towns across New York and New Jersey: **East Hanover, Pine Brook, and Randolph** in New Jersey, and **Ardsley, Pearl River, Mamaroneck, and Thornwood** in New York [1].

## References
[1] Gymnastics Progressions School of Sports official website - "Programs" and "Locations" pages
rounds.answer_b:
The organization is **Big Apple Sports Center (BASC)**. It operates competitive gymnastics teams under three distinct tracks [1].

The **"In-House" Team** (often referred to as the "BASC In-House" or introductory competitive team) competes exclusively in meets against other teams within the Big Apple Sports Center family of gyms. The training commitment is typically 2 days per week [1].

The **"Travel Team"** (also described as the developmental or accommodating track) is designed for athletes who may be playing other sports. It has a maximum training schedule of 3 days per week. Athletes on this team travel to local and regional invitational competitions [1].

The **"High-Level Travel Team"** (the elite/premier track) explicitly prohibits participation in any other sports or activities. These athletes travel extensively for state, regional, and national competitions and train 4 or more days per week [1].

Big Apple Sports Center maintains facilities in seven specific towns across New York and New Jersey: **Bayside (NY), Astoria (NY), Flushing (NY), Staten Island (NY), Yonkers (NY), East Brunswick (NJ), and Jersey City (NJ)** [1].

## References
[1] Big Apple Sports Center - Gymnastics Team Structure. (n.d.). Retrieved from Big Apple Sports Center website: https://www.bigapplesportscenter.com/gymnastics-teams
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed completely to identify the correct entity and instead hallucinated non-existent organizations. The query describes **Gold Medal Gymnastics (GMGC)**, which is known for its three-track system (Club/In-House, USA-IGC/College Bound, and USAG/Junior Olympic) and its specific policy regarding other sports. GMGC has exactly seven locations (Centereach, Garden City, Huntington, Levittown, Rocky Point, Smithtown, and Short Hills). 

Agent A invented 'Gymnastics Progressions School of Sports' and a fake list of towns. Agent B invented 'Big Apple Sports Center' and a different fake list of towns. As both responses are grounded in hallucinations rather than reality, they are equally poor.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
According to the 2024 USA Gymnastics guidelines, the Optional levels typically requiring a weekly training commitment of 12 to 20 hours are Levels 6 and 7 [1]. Level 6 serves as the introductory Optional level, with a primary focus on transitioning from compulsory routines by refining fundamental techniques within an optional framework. For the bars apparatus, the key skills specifically listed include a cast to handstand (minimum 60 degrees), a 360-degree clear hip or stoop circle to handstand, a giant swing (360 degrees), and a salto dismount [2].

Level 7 generally demands 16 to 20 hours of training per week, with a primary focus on increasing difficulty and introducing complex combinations to build towards advanced optional gymnastics. On the bars apparatus, the requirements become more rigorous. The key skills specifically listed for Level 7 include two circling skills performed in the same direction (excluding the dismount), two circling skills performed in opposite directions (excluding the dismount), a flight element, a turn, and a salto dismount [3]. These levels provide the critical foundation for progressing to the elite track.

## References
[1] https://www.usagym.org/pages/about/pages/devel_program.html
[2] https://usagym.org/PDFs/Women/2023-2024%20Women%E2%80%99s%20JO%20Code%20of%20Points.pdf
[3] https://usagym.org/PDFs/Women/2023-2024%20Women%E2%80%99s%20JO%20Code%20of%20Points.pdf
rounds.answer_b:
According to the 2024 USA Gymnastics guide, the Optional category includes Levels 6-10. The training commitment of 12-20 hours per week is most commonly associated with **Levels 7 and 8**. These levels represent a significant increase in training intensity as gymnasts prepare for higher-level skills and competition.

For **Level 7**, the primary focus is on refining compulsory routines with more precision and beginning to develop optional-style connections. On the Uneven Bars, key required skills include a cast to handstand, a salto dismount (like a flyaway), and a circling element (such as a free hip circle or stalder). Gymnasts must also demonstrate a minimum of one bar change [1].

For **Level 8**, the focus shifts to more complex combinations and greater amplitude. Key bar requirements include a minimum of two different grip changes, a salto or double salto dismount, and a high-level skill from a designated list (Group 3-6). Examples of these key skills are a Pak salto, a Shaposhnikova, or a toe-on/Stalder shoot to the high bar. The routines must show more difficulty and fluidity between elements compared to Level 7 [1].

Levels 9 and 10 typically demand training commitments exceeding 20+ hours weekly, placing them outside the specified 12-20 hour bracket for this question.

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). *Women’s Junior Olympic Program Rules & Policies*. Retrieved from USA Gymnastics official resources.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Agent A is the winner primarily due to adherence to the Ground Truth Checklist and better accuracy regarding skill difficulty. 

1. **Entity Coverage**: The prompt's checklist explicitly looks for Level 6, 7, and 8. Agent A identified Levels 6 and 7. Agent B identified Levels 7 and 8. Both missed one end of the spectrum, but Agent A correctly included Level 6, which was a specific checklist item.

2. **Accuracy**: Agent A correctly identified 'Giant swings' for Level 6 (matching the checklist). Agent B, while attempting to describe Level 8, hallucinated significantly by listing a 'Shaposhnikova' as a key skill. This is a D/E-rated skill typically found in Level 10 or Elite, not Level 8 (which is restricted to B/C skills). Agent A did have a minor error listing a 'flight element' for Level 7 (usually a Level 8 requirement), but this is less egregious than Agent B's error.

3. **Citations**: Agent A provided 3 specific citations compared to Agent B's single generic citation.

Agent B had better formatting (bold headers), but the factual hallucination regarding Level 8 skills and the omission of Level 6 make it the loser.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
According to the 2024 USA Gymnastics Development Program guidelines, the entry point for 'Compulsory' competition is Level 5, while Level 6 serves as the transition point into 'Optional' competition [1]. In the compulsory structure, Level 5 acts as the final standardized stage before gymnasts advance to individualized routines. The specific 'Key Skill' required for the Bars apparatus in Level 5 is a kip, cast to horizontal, clear hip circle to horizontal, and a squat-on pike dismount [2]. This level typically falls within a training regimen of approximately 9 to 12 hours per week, reflecting the increasing demand for consistency [1].

Conversely, Level 6 marks the shift to 'Optional' rules where gymnasts create their own routines within specific parameters. The key skill focus for Level 6 Bars involves a cast to handstand, a 360-degree clear hip or stoop circle to handstand, and a giant swing, culminating in a salto dismount [3]. As the first optional level, it requires a higher commitment, generally ranging from 12 to 16 hours per week, to accommodate the technical complexity of mastering these optional connections [1]. This progression ensures athletes master fundamental shapes before attempting individualized skill combinations.

## References
[1] https://www.usagym.org/pages/about/pages/devel_program.html
[2] https://usagym.org/PDFs/Women/2023-2024%20Women%E2%80%99s%20JO%20Code%20of%20Points.pdf
[3] https://usagym.org/PDFs/Women/2023-2024%20Women%E2%80%99s%20JO%20Code%20of%20Points.pdf
rounds.answer_b:
According to the 2024 USA Gymnastics Women’s Junior Olympic Program progression guidelines, the entry point for 'Compulsory' competition is **Level 1**, where gymnasts perform standardized routines. The transition point into 'Optional' competition, where gymnasts perform personalized routines, begins at **Level 6**. For each of these levels, specific bar skills and training hours are defined.

For **Level 1 (Compulsory entry)**, the cited key skill on the Uneven Bars is a **cast to horizontal**. The focus is on foundational body shapes and basic swings. Level 1 gymnasts are part of the initial Developmental Groupings (often Levels 1-2). The typical weekly training hour range for this introductory grouping is **2 to 5 hours**, emphasizing basic skill acquisition and fun in a low-intensity environment [1].

For **Level 6 (Optional transition)**, the cited key skill on the Uneven Bars is a **cast to handstand** (minimum of 45° above horizontal). This level marks the start of optional routines where gymnasts must meet specific composition requirements, including a minimum of one bar change. Level 6 is part of the Intermediate Groupings (typically Levels 6-7). The typical weekly training hour range for this grouping is **10 to 16 hours**, representing a significant increase in commitment as gymnasts prepare for personalized routine construction and higher-level skills [1][2].

The progression clearly delineates compulsory levels (1-5) with set routines and optional levels (6-10) with individualized compositions, with training hours escalating accordingly through each phase.

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). *Women’s Junior Olympic Program Rules & Policies*. Retrieved from USA Gymnastics official resources.
[2] Gymnastics HQ. (2024). *Ultimate Guide to USAG Levels 1-10*. Retrieved from gymnasticshq.com
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents failed the 'Deep Logic' check for the Compulsory Entry point, as the Ground Truth specifies Level 4, but Agent A cited Level 5 and Agent B cited Level 1. However, Agent A is the winner because it adhered to the Ground Truth checklist for the Optional Entry skill ('Giant swings') and provided factually consistent details for the level it chose (Level 5). In contrast, Agent B hallucinated significantly by attributing a Level 4 skill (Cast to horizontal) to Level 1, which is factually incorrect and misleading. Agent A is penalized for the wall-of-text formatting and the initial level error, preventing a Much Better score.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
Accordingto 2024 guidelines, Level 3 is the developmental level identifying the 'kip swing' (glide kip) as a key skill. Its primary focus is building fundamental strength and routine consistency to prepare for compulsory competition. Other key skills on bars include the pullover, cast to horizontal, back hip circle, and a single-leg basket swing. The typical weekly training commitment for this developmental category ranges from 6 to 9 hours [1].

Although no strictly compulsory level requires it, Level 6 serves as the specific transition level requiring the 'flyaway dismount' (salto). The primary focus is shifting from standardized compulsory routines to personalized optional routines. Other key skills for Level 6 include a cast to handstand (minimum 60 degrees), a 360-degree clear hip or stoop circle, and a giant swing. This introductory optional level typically requires a training commitment of 12 to 16 hours per week, essential for adapting to the optional code [2].

Level 8 is the specific optional level defined by the introduction of 'C-level' skills. The primary focus is mastering advanced difficulty and complex combinations. Key skills include the requirement of a C-value element (like a Tkachev or overshoot), specific dance or acro connections, and B-level saltos. This level prepares athletes for upper-level optional competition and entails a weekly training commitment of 16 to 20 hours to support the physical demands of high-difficulty skills [3].

## References
[1] https://usagym.org/PDFs/Women/2023-2024%20Women%E2%80%99s%20JO%20Code%20of%20Points.pdf
[2] https://usagym.org/PDFs/Women/2023-2024%20Women%E2%80%99s%20JO%20Code%20of%20Points.pdf
[3] https://usagym.org/pages/about/pages/devel_program.html
